title: Greater Boston Housing Supply Analysis
description: This exploratory data analysis examines Greater Boston's housing supply crisis through the lens of zoning, income, and educational rankings. The project identifies five areas (Nahant, Swampscott, Melrose, Norwood, and Arlington) with single-family-only zoning and high concentrations of low-income households as top priorities for upzoning, finds mixed evidence that historical restrictive zoning still concentrates BIMPOC populations in non-restrictive zones, and reveals a tentative pattern where higher-ranked school districts correlate with higher-income households.
story: I started this project expecting to find clear villains in Boston's housing crisis — maybe a few suburbs clinging to exclusionary zoning out of pure self-interest. What I found instead was a web of interconnected incentives where school rankings, property values, and zoning policy all reinforce each other. The five towns I flagged as top upzoning priorities weren't simply wealthy enclaves — they were places where the gap between stated affordability goals and actual land-use policy was widest. That tension between intent and outcome is what I keep coming back to.

title: COVID-19 Visualization Critique & Redesign
description: This project critiques the New York Times' COVID-19 spiral visualization, identifying its lack of a common quantitative datum and insufficient month labeling as key weaknesses despite its attention-grabbing design. Through three exploratory sketches, the analysis explores trade-offs between visual appeal and readability before arriving at a final overlapping area chart design that prioritizes accurate monthly comparison across 2020-2022.
story: The NYT spiral was one of those visualizations that made me stop scrolling — it was beautiful, and I had no idea what it was saying. That friction sent me down a rabbit hole about what visualization is actually for. My redesign traded novelty for clarity: a shared zero baseline, full month labels, and a three-year average line that made the seasonal pattern visible for the first time. Sometimes the most honest design choice is also the least exciting one.

title: Persuasion vs. Deception in Data Visualization
description: This project explores the boundary between persuasive and deceptive visualization by creating two opposing arguments about whether the world economy is in a mature phase. The 'FOR' visualization uses a logarithmic scale and narrow time window to suggest stability, while the 'AGAINST' visualization employs a linear scale and extended timeline to emphasize accelerating growth.
story: The assignment asked me to argue both sides using the same dataset, and I was surprised by how easy it was. Choosing a log scale versus a linear one, or starting a chart in 2015 versus 1956, are defensible technical decisions — but they can completely flip the story. The uncomfortable conclusion I landed on: most visualization 'best practices' are themselves rhetorical choices, and the line between persuasion and deception is thinner than we like to admit.

title: Toyota Body Engineering
description: Promoted underbody structure sharing with 2 types of vehicles across 3 vehicle companies to optimize structure of installed parts, creating information sharing system between related departments using Toyota Production System approach, executing agile development with overall vehicle planning departments and vehicle body performance evaluation departments.
story: Standardization sounds straightforward until you're in a room with engineers from three vehicle companies who each have excellent reasons why their variant is the one that should become the standard. This project taught me that the hardest part of platform engineering isn't the geometry — it's building enough shared context that people can argue about trade-offs rather than talking past each other. The patent came out of a discussion with various people where we finally found the geometry that satisfied everyone's constraints.

title: Ford Strategy & Planning Leadership Program for Advanced EV Development
description: Created a new holistic package of supplier sourcing documents from scratch to discover new suppliers for projects and simplify the process based on the first principles thinking, resulting in an 87% content reduction while maintaining its efficiency. Completed a concept paper submission for a tax credit application based on competitive benchmarking with cross-functional departments, such as engineering, marketing, finance, and manufacturing, anticipating its value of more than hundreds of millions for the project. Invented a new product development process concept to consider to work in ambiguity with agility: Phased Agile with Rigid Gate.
story: I came into the Ford program expecting to work on EV hardware — and instead spent most of my time redesigning the processes around it. The supplier sourcing documents we inherited were built for a slower era: dense, repetitive, and impossible to navigate for suppliers who hadn't worked with Ford before. Cutting 87% of the content while keeping the process intact forced me to ask what each requirement was actually for. The Phased Agile with Rigid Gate concept came out of that same frustration — the team needed to move fast in early stages, but the downstream manufacturing commitments meant we couldn't afford to replay decisions. The framework was an attempt to protect both.

title: MIT Driverless Autonomous Racing System
description: As Co-GM of MIT Driverless, led a 28-member team developing a full autonomous racing stack for the Abu Dhabi Autonomous Racing League. Oversaw vehicle architecture, sensor fusion pipeline design, and team operations across mechanical, electrical, and software subteams.
story: Running an autonomous racing team is equal parts engineering and organizational design. The technical problems are hard — reliably detecting track boundaries at 150 km/h in desert light conditions is not a solved problem — but the harder challenge is keeping 28 people aligned when they're working in parallel on systems that depend on each other. I spent as much time on meeting structure and documentation standards as I did on the vehicle itself, and I think that's the right trade-off.

title: Apple Park Product Architecture Residency
description: As an MIT-Riccio Engineering Leadership Resident, embedded with Apple's product architecture team to work on early-stage design for next-generation products. Focused on cross-functional coordination between design, engineering, and supply chain during concept-phase decision making.
story: The thing that surprised me most about Apple wasn't the secrecy — it was how much ambiguity exists at the concept stage of a product that millions of people will eventually hold in their hands. My job was to help structure the decision process for problems that didn't yet have well-defined requirements. That experience reframed how I think about engineering leadership: the highest-leverage moment isn't optimizing a design, it's asking the right question before the design space closes.

title: Engineering Workflow Startup
description: Founding a startup applying Toyota Production System principles to engineering design workflows. Building tools that help product teams eliminate coordination waste, surface bottlenecks earlier, and give engineers more time for work that actually matters.
story: Every place I've worked, the engineers I most respected spent a surprising fraction of their time on coordination overhead — tracking down the latest file version, figuring out who owns a decision, re-explaining context that should have been written down. I've been thinking about this problem for years, and I finally have a clear enough point of view to build something. The insight from Toyota is that waste in a design process is just as real as waste on a factory floor — it's just harder to see.

title: Boston Marathon Qualification
description: Trained for and completed a qualifying marathon with a 2:53:25 finish, securing a Boston Marathon qualifier. Developed a structured 18-week training plan balancing high-mileage weeks with engineering coursework and team leadership responsibilities at MIT.
story: I started running seriously during a period when most of my work felt abstract — a lot of planning, coordination, and strategic thinking without much tangible output. Running gave me something the rest of my life wasn't: an immediate, unambiguous measurement of progress. The 2:53 came after three years of consistent training and two failed qualifier attempts. The lesson I keep carrying back to work is that compounding matters more than intensity — the athletes who run every day at moderate effort almost always beat the ones who train in spikes.
